sentences.text: My name is Peter Leopold Fischl.
And it's spelled P-E-T-E-R L-E-O-P-O-L-D F-I-S-C-H-L.
And actually, that's in English.
In-- and-- and the nickname-- my nickname in-- in-- in America would be Pete, P-E-T-E.
In Hungary, my name would be Fischl Peter, Fischl Leopold Peter.
And my-- my nickname in-- in Hungary would be not Pete, but-- I don't know what my nickname would be.
I did, but I forget now.
I was born in July 19, 1930, in Budapest, Hungary.
And what was the next question?
So--
Generally speaking it was great.
It was great.
My father was a very, very successful businessmen, and we lived a very, very high middle-class life, you would say in America.
And our [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] chauffeur would take us to school and pick us up and take us to the horses for horse riding and things like that.
And every year, we would take vacations in surrounding countries, visit.
Tibor, which spells T-I-B-O-R.
Tibor is the-- is the name of my father, was the name of my father.
Was Edith, E-D-I-T-H.
My mother was a housewife.
And she was a daughter of a, a doctor.
My grandfather was a doctor, a professor here in a hospital.
He was an ear, nose, throat specialist.
And so when my mother married my father, that she became a housewife.
And then we were born shortly, and she took care of us.
My sister.
I have a sister.
Marika, Maria is her name, my sister's name.
As far as religion is concerned, no, we were not religious.
My grandmother was religious.
So many times my father would take us over to my grandmother's place, and my sister and I would stay there on the weekend or something.
Then we would go through all the rituals, and all the religious rituals.
But other than that, in our home, we weren't religious at all.
We, we were not.
We did not go to synagogues on a weekly basis.
Once in a great while we would, but not on a regular basis, we would not.
As a child, one time I watched my father-- we had a piano at home.
And my father liked to play the piano.
And at eight, seven, I was, I was watching my father play that old 'Sonny Boy' tune that's from a very old, I think 1930, American-- the first sound movie, I think, in America.
Al Jolson, Al Jolson was singing that tune.
[SINGING]
And I was listening to that, and I said to my father, I said, jeez, I like this very much.
Can I learn that?
And he said, certainly.
So I sat next to him, and that's how I started playing the piano.
And I copied him and played that tune.
And then many times when I was alone, I would play for hours, the same tune over and over and over.
And it didn't bother me.
It probably bothered the neighbors, but it didn't bother me.
And then I started learning other songs.
And interestingly enough, if I liked a tune or something, I was able, by trial and error, to play another tune by myself.
And so piano was one.
And, and another thing was at my grandmother's place, she had a chess board.
And she taught, she taught me how to set up chess figures.
And so at a very early age, I started playing chess.
Oh, about seven.
And I, and I learned how to move correctly.
And I played with people.
And it enter-- entertained me a lot.
So that's how I think in my life I learned enjoying challenges a lot from very early childhood.
Playing complicated things, doing complicated things, but enjoying it a lot.
I had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a gentlemen who was about 12 years older-- my grandmother hired him to be my home teacher and to, to, to teach me things.
And, and, and, and his first this was, which at age seven or so, he taught me a poem in Hungarian.
[HUNGARIAN] And that was about 50 years, 58 years ago.
And I still remember that.
That gentlemen now in Canada.
He's a literature professor and teaches in college.
And I just wrote to him, isn't it interesting that I have learned my first poem from you.
Certainly.
It says, the [NON-ENGLISH] is the person who's watching the animals, and he's sitting on a donkey.
And he says, [HUNGARIAN].
And his legs are touching the ground.
And that's what it means.
And it's about this [NON-ENGLISH], this keeper who watches-- a cowboy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That what this poem about.
I don't remember the whole poem, but I remember that much.
And we had a lot of active things.
My father exposed us to a lot of things.
We traveled a lot at an early age.
Every year-- every year we went to Italy to-- to-- for summertime.
And so I loved the train.
I loved to train.
I loved it.
I loved that rhythm of the way the tracks are put together.
And they don't fit perfectly, and it gives a certain noise.
And I love it.
I love it.
And it has a rhythm.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I, I, I-- just traveling on-- just sitting in a train, I enjoyed it.

Probably being by myself or not too many people around me and then seeing the scenery which changes, watching the people and the scenery and thinking on who these people could be and what are they doing.
And it was very entertaining for me.
And then-- so-- Then sometimes we would go to Czechoslovakia, which is also surrounding country of Hungary, and see things and experience things.
To see things.
To see things.
We had a very close family in a sense.
My grandfather had a huge, enormous huge home in [INAUDIBLE] in Budapest.
And then every weekend the whole family would meet there, once a week almost.
And he had enormous, big balcony he had.
And then the whole family would sit there, and we would have breakfast there, and we would talk with each other.
And it was really beautiful, you know.
That-- it was peaceful and beautiful.
And--
In Hungary, I went to an elementary school.
It's a different system like, like in America.
In Hungary, you go four years to an elementary school.
And after, you switch to a school what here you would call maybe a high school kind of thing.
But it's different because over there, eight years-- now, after the four years you are finished, you go eight years into the same school.
And then you graduate there, and then you go to college if you want to and so on.
So I went to the [INAUDIBLE] Jewish School in Hungary.
Did I have friends in school?
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
I had some friends, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And some of them died.
And that was very sad, after the war when I discovered that they died.
But when I went to school, I had a family-- we knew-- my father knew-- my father knew that family called the [?
Landesmant ?] family.
And they had about four or five members of that family going to that school.
And they were very close friends of mine.
Because of family ties through my parents, they were friends, so we were very close friends.
But basically I was a very open kid, and I was friendly to almost everybody.
Almost everybody.
Sometimes cruel, very cruel, but friendly.
Sometimes I made very cruel practical jokes in school.
There was, in my days-- I don't know, do you want to hear it?
Yes, please.
In my days, we had for the ink what we were writing, we had a little ink holder.
It looked like a top hat turned around.
And that's where the ink was in.
And then we had a pen.
You had to immerse the pen and then write with it.
And there was also plenty of empty, these ink holders, no ink in them.
And, and one of my friends next to me, sitting next to me, was clowning and picking up these empty ink holders and putting them on his head, like he would be saluting to people with these top hats.
And I switched one of these empties with one which was full with ink.
And then he picked it up and put it on his head.
And he didn't realize what's happening.
And he was just keep on going.
And the teacher didn't see what's happening.
And then slowly the ink started pouring out on his face.
He still didn't realize it.
And then the whole class started laughing enormously.
And then naturally the teacher stopped it and realized it.
And then he had to go out and wash himself.
And he was a close and very good-hearted friend.
But he was like the son of Frankenstein, a huge person, about four times bigger than I was.
And after the class was over, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he chased me.
And he caught up with me, and he picked me up, and he pushed me against the wall.
And then he let me go, and then I just fell down.
He really didn't hurt me, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but just was very angry for a very good reason.
So I went home and everything and I was in bed when-- and it was late, and my mother said-- I was almost getting ready to sleep.
And my mother said, somebody's calling you.
And his name was Steiner.
And Steiner-- Robert Steiner was his name.
And he was calling me and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he cried on the telephone.
And he said, I hope I didn't hurt you when I threw you against the wall.
And this is something so beautiful that I won't forget as long as I live.
And more so because after the war was over I met his mother.
And I said to her mother-- they lived very close where we lived-- I asked her mother, I said, where is Robbie?
And she said, well, didn't you know?
I said, no.
Know what?
And Robbie survived.
And after the war, after the war for us in Budapest, the war wasn't over, his mother sent him out to get bread.
And he went out to get bread, and a small bomb hit him and tore him into pieces, and he died.
And that's how I found out that he died.
That was in 1945, early 1945.
Because we were liberated in January 1945, and so that was after the liberation or so.
And so I never going to forget Robbie, my friend.
And I hope he's listening to me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] many, many people in the school I, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't know.
But it was an interesting thing also, an experience that-- because there was not enough room.
We had to switch.
So there were girls separately and boys going separately in the same classes.
But let's say the boys would be going from 8:00 in the morning till noon, and the girls would come at 12:30 and be till 4:00.
And one day-- one day, I left my book in-- in-- in my place where I was sitting.
And my name was written in it, Peter Fischl.
And so the a girl who sat in the same place knew who sits there.

And she carved in, heaven knows why, she carved in this wooden [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chair in the writing arrangement-- I don't know if you're familiar with it, old European school system where you sit and then here is where you write.
And everything is made out of wood.
And she carved in that-- she carved in a heart and she said-- she wrote in-- carved in, 'I love you, Peter.' And, and till this day, I'm thinking who Renee could have been.
And many times, I'm thinking was-- that I should have maybe made a harder effort to search for her, which I did not.
It's kind of the mystery woman in my life and the mystery person in my life.
Oh, probably 12 or 13.
And then [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] because maybe I'm thinking of her a lot, maybe because she was only person who just sent love towards me.
And so that was something, an interesting thing.
Well, because in life-- because in life, you meet people and then you fall in love and then later on disappointments start coming and-- because nobody's perfect and so on.
So then relationships weaken, marriages are breaking up, like mine.
And with her, there was nothing like that because that's the only experience we had is just this one writing experience.
I did not know where she-- who she was.
No, never.
Never.
Never.
But it-- maybe it's not too late to-- to do a search.
And naturally, I still see her as a certain, you know, girl in my mind, because I cannot accept that-- my mind cannot accept 52 years which went by.
And the school was a very good school, very interesting.
I learned a lot of things.
I think that was just a matter of chance, and heaven knows why those little ink holders were empty and they were there.
I have no idea.
Maybe-- it could been that maybe not in every space people were sitting.
And only maybe they filled up the places with ink where somebody was sitting and writing.
Maybe Robbie pulled it out himself.
I don't remember that detail.
But I just remember the fact that he had these empties around him and he was clowning.
That's when my mind started working and doing this thing towards him.
My sister is about 2 and 1/2 years older than I am.
So basically I lived a very lonely life as a child, because my sister was a very attractive young lady.
And so at age 12, 13, there was a lot of boys who were interested in her and came up to our house and sat around there.
And sometimes there was 10, 15 boys sitting there just to be around my sister and talk with her.
And nobody was really interested in me.
And my father was a very busy businessman and under tremendous pressure.
I really don't recall a lot of things, a lot of times where he really would have been with me and with us really.
I, I, I just remember sometimes dinners and breakfasts and, and lunches.
But I really don't remember that my father and I would have, what they say here in America, would have went fishing.
I never went fishing with my father.
I could write a book about this.
Well, my grandmother and my grandfather came from a small town where they worked themself up into business.
And so my-- when my father was a little boy, they-- his parents were very well doing already.
And so my grandmother made it sure that my father goes into a good school and meets the right people.
And then he set my father up in business.
Actually, my grandfather had a business called Leopold Fischl and Son.
And their business is something which, to my knowledge, is-- it's probably around in America, too, or was, but I never heard of it.
Is a person who works with the large farmers and--
Let's say, here the government helps the farmers, the medium farmers and large farmers.

In, in, in Hungary, my father would be like working with these medium and large farming people, and let's say buying the livestock a year or two years ahead of the time before they really want to sell it.
So actually they worked with my father's money.
And then once the livestock grew up, then, then it belonged to my father.
And they made their profit, and then my father would resell it, export it also.
My father exported to Israel also.
And matter of fact, matter of fact, my father gave some livestock as gifts to Israel to a kibbutz in Israel.
And I don't remember now the name, but I think it was a kibbutz where Hannah Senesh wanted to go, I think.
You heard of Hannah Senesh.
Well, that was before she escaped from Hungary and got captured.
That was done in probably in 1938, 1938 or so.
At that time in Israel, they had problems with the British.
And so this was part of my father's business.
But my father was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] doing anything besides this, which-- he was what you would call, like, an entrepreneur.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He made a lot of money, but if somebody would come in and would offer him a apartment building, a hundred-unit apartment building for a good price, then he would buy it.
And, ant that's how I could describe him.
He was a very creative person.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But he also took a lot of chances in, in-- he never lost his money, but he took a lot of chances in his business dealings.
So he was under tremendous pressure, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in-- I could see.
